Bitcoin is a monetary asset optimized for portability, divisibility, and seizure resistance. Comparing them on the basis of 'can you live in it' is like comparing gold to a house. Both can be excellent investments. Neither is the other.

The assumption that physical property is safe from government action is historically naive. In Argentina's 2001 financial crisis, bank accounts were frozen and real estate could not be sold for reasonable prices. In Venezuela, hyperinflation made the peso-denominated value of property worthless while the bricks remained. Property taxes, eminent domain, rent control, and rezoning regularly reduce the value and utility of physical real estate against the owner's wishes.

Real estate is catastrophically illiquid. Selling a house takes 60–90 days under favorable conditions, involves 5–10% in transaction costs, and requires a willing buyer. Bitcoin can be sold globally, 24/7, 365 days a year, in any amount, at market price, with settlement in minutes. For any scenario requiring rapid access to value — medical emergency, relocation, capital controls — Bitcoin's liquidity is real estate's weakness made stark.

For the billions of people globally who cannot afford real estate — or who live in countries where property rights are poorly enforced — Bitcoin provides the ability to hold monetary value securely that real estate simply cannot provide. A Venezuelan or Nigerian does not have the option of 'just buy a house.' They do have the option of holding Bitcoin.

The comparison also runs the other direction: Bitcoin in self-custody cannot be confiscated via eminent domain, cannot be seized by a landlord dispute, cannot be affected by a neighbor's environmental violation, and cannot be foreclosed. The seizure resistance of Bitcoin versus the numerous ways physical property can be taken should be part of the comparison.
keyTakeaway: Bitcoin and housing are not substitutes — they are complements that serve different functions. The inability to live in Bitcoin proves only that it is money, not shelter. Money's job is to preserve exchange value and remain transferable. Bitcoin does this with properties physical real estate cannot match: instant global transfer, seizure resistance, and perfect divisibility.

title: Bitcoin is slower than Visa — it can't compete as a payment network
subtitle: Comparing Bitcoin's settlement layer to Visa's authorization layer is comparing apples to the concept of fruit
openingLine: Visa does not settle transactions. It authorizes them. The actual settlement — moving real money between banks — takes 2–3 days and is handled by networks that process far fewer transactions. Bitcoin settles final, irreversible transactions in 10 minutes. Visa's network cannot do that at any speed.

stats.label: Bitcoin base layer — final, irreversible settlement
Lightning Network theoretical throughput
Actual bank settlement time behind Visa transactions
stats.note: Visa's 24,000 TPS is authorization, not settlement

whyPeopleBelieve: Visa processes up to 65,000 transactions per second. Bitcoin's base layer handles approximately 7. This seems like a decisive technological inferiority that disqualifies Bitcoin from being a global payment system.
supportingArguments: Bitcoin's 7 TPS cannot handle global commerce volume at any meaningful scale.
Transaction confirmation times of 10+ minutes are impractical for point-of-sale retail.
Network congestion causes fees to spike, pricing out small transactions during peak periods.
counterArguments: Visa does not settle money — it authorizes charges. When you swipe your Visa card, the actual movement of funds between your bank and the merchant's bank takes 2–3 business days through ACH or Fedwire. Visa's '24,000 TPS' is the speed of recording IOUs, not the speed of final settlement. Bitcoin's base layer performs final, irreversible settlement with no counterparty risk. These are not the same function.

Bitcoin's payment layer — the Lightning Network — processes transactions at millions per second, with settlement in under a second, at fees measured in fractions of a cent. Lightning is already operational. Strike processes international payments instantly. El Salvador's national payment system runs on Lightning. The speed limitation critics cite applies to the base settlement layer, not the payment layer built on top of it.

Gold's settlement network processes roughly 6–10 major transactions per day — the movement of physical gold between vaults — and takes days to weeks. No one argues gold is 'too slow' as a monetary base. The relevant comparison for Bitcoin's base layer is not Visa. It is gold, Federal Reserve Fedwire, and SWIFT — and Bitcoin's 10-minute finality is faster and more final than all of them.

Bitcoin's base layer block space is deliberately scarce to preserve decentralization. The design choice is: accept slower base-layer throughput to maintain a system that 50,000+ nodes globally can validate without specialized hardware. The tradeoff produces a maximally decentralized and censorship-resistant settlement layer — properties that Visa's network is specifically designed not to have.

For the use cases where Bitcoin's speed matters most — large international transfers, cross-border business payments, remittances — 10-minute settlement is already faster than wire transfers, which take 1–5 business days and require banking relationships in both countries. For everyday retail payments, Lightning solves the speed problem entirely.
keyTakeaway: Bitcoin is not designed to compete with Visa's authorization layer. It is designed to be the world's final settlement layer — the monetary base upon which faster payment systems are built. Lightning provides Visa-speed payments on top of Bitcoin. The architecture is layers, not a single slow network. The comparison was always wrong.
